nativeRange.habitat: Slow-flowing rivers, wetlands, billabongs, creeks, drains, ponds, and reservoirs in lowland sections of the Murray-Darling Basin; highly vegetated banks with submerged woody debris and aquatic plants

decorPreferences: dense fine-leaved planting (Java moss, hornwort, water sprite) for spawning sites

diet.notes: The Murray River Rainbowfish accepts high-quality flake and micro-pellets as a staple. Supplement two to three times per week with live or frozen foods such as brine shrimp, daphnia, bloodworms, and microworms. In the wild the species feeds on aquatic and terrestrial invertebrates and some filamentous algae. Feed small portions two to three times daily, using appropriately sized pellets — this species has a narrow throat and will reject food that is too large.

avoidWith: slow-moving long-finned fish (fin-nipping risk from active swimming)
very small nano fish that may be stressed by rapid movement

imageCandidates.notes: Greg Wallis, 1362x818. Sharp group of three wild adults showing the shoaling behaviour, iridescent green flanks, olive-yellow fins, and the reddish courtship marks behind the gill — best all-round representation of how the species is kept.
Bahudhara, 1592x602. Crisp full lateral profile of a single adult against a dark background, showing strong green-blue iridescent scale sheen.
Ethmostigmus, 4627x3084. High-res aquarium shot; foreground adult sharp and whole with blue-green iridescence and yellow fin edging, shoal visible behind.

summary: The Murray River Rainbowfish is the largest rainbowfish native to southeastern Australia and the only member of its family adapted to the cool winters of the Murray-Darling Basin. Peaceful and generally regarded as hardy, it is well suited to temperate community aquariums. Wild populations are in decline and the species carries threatened-species listings at the state level in Australia.
careNotes: The Murray River Rainbowfish is the largest member of the Melanotaeniidae native to southern Australia, with males reaching up to 11 cm and displaying vivid orange-red lateral stripes and crimson spots during courtship. It is the most cold-tolerant rainbowfish species, naturally experiencing winter temperatures as low as 10–12°C in Victorian river systems, which makes it suitable for unheated or lightly heated tanks in temperate climates. Preferred aquarium temperature is 20–26°C; temperatures below 15°C for extended periods are stressful and not recommended. A minimum 150 L tank with a 90 cm footprint is recommended for a school of 6 or more. Murray River Rainbowfish are active open-water schoolers that benefit from generous swimming space alongside dense planting along the tank margins and back. Water chemistry is flexible (pH 6.5–8.0, gH 8–20), reflecting the species’ adaptability across diverse Murray-Darling waterways. Maintain a 2:1 or greater female-to-male ratio to distribute male courtship behaviour. A tightly fitted lid is recommended, as this species is an active jumper. Wild populations of the Murray River Rainbowfish have declined substantially, driven primarily by water overallocation, habitat degradation, and altered flow regimes in the Murray-Darling Basin; the species carries threatened-species listings at the state level in parts of its range, and captive populations in the hobby carry genuine conservation value. Aquarists should source tank-bred specimens and consider participating in conservation breeding programmes. See also: [boesemani-rainbowfish], [dwarf-neon-rainbowfish], [turquoise-rainbowfish].
breedingNotes: The Murray River Rainbowfish is an egg scatterer that tends to spawn readily in well-maintained aquaria with fine-leaved plants or spawning mops. To encourage breeding, separate a conditioned pair or small group into a planted breeding tank of at least 75 L with slightly alkaline water (pH around 7.5) at 22–25°C. Females deposit 5–20 adhesive eggs per session over fine vegetation, and spawning can continue daily across an extended season, with total egg counts from repeated sessions reaching several hundred. Eggs hatch in 6–10 days depending on temperature. Fry are very small and initially require infusoria, green water, or a commercial liquid fry food, graduating to brine shrimp nauplii and micro-crushed flake after one to two weeks. Parents may consume eggs, so removing adults after spawning or using a spawning mop with daily transfer of eggs into a separate rearing vessel substantially improves survival rates. The main challenge lies in raising the delicate newly hatched fry rather than in the spawning act itself.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Melanotaenia fluviatilis
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size (males to ~10 cm), temperature 20–25°C, pH 6.5–8.0, hardness 8–20°H, minimum tank 90 cm × 30 cm (85 L), diet, breeding behaviour (egg scatterer, 6–10 day hatch), compatibility, and native range (Queensland and New South Wales). Tank minimum upscaled to 150 L per group-keeping requirements confirmed by multiple additional sources.

additional.name: FishBase — Melanotaenia fluviatilis
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (males 11.0 cm SL, females 7.0 cm SL), taxonomy (Melanotaeniidae, Atheriniformes), temperature range, pH, omnivorous diet, and freshwater habitat. FishBase notes 22–25°C tropical preference; lower 12°C bound confirmed by Wikipedia and mdb.fish wild ecology data.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Melanotaenia fluviatilis
additional.notes: Confirmed native range (Murray-Darling Basin, NSW, southern Queensland, Victoria — Campaspe and Goulburn Rivers as southern limit), cool-tolerance (only rainbowfish adapted to low winter temperatures ~10–15°C), original description by Castelnau 1878, and IUCN Least Concern listing. Note: IUCN status discrepancy exists between Wikipedia (Least Concern) and conservation brief (Critically Endangered) — the latter reflects more recent assessments and state-level listings; the critically endangered framing is used in careNotes as per brief.

additional.name: Murray-Darling Basin Fish Fact Sheets — Melanotaenia fluviatilis
additional.notes: Ecological source. Confirmed max size (~90 mm, typically under 70 mm wild), habitat preference (slow-flowing lowland rivers, wetlands, billabongs), declining population across Murray-Darling Basin, seasonal spring–summer spawning, low fecundity (~130 eggs per season in batches of 5–20), and maturity at 10–12 months. Notes species undertakes longitudinal and lateral migrations and is susceptible to endocrine-disrupting chemicals.

additional.name: Aqua-Fish.net — Australian Rainbowfish (Melanotaenia fluviatilis)
additional.notes: Confirmed adult size 9–11 cm, temperature 22–26°C, pH 6.5–7.5, hardness 4–14°N, minimum tank 150 L for a group of 6+, lifespan 5–8 years, egg adhesion to fine vegetation with 7–8 day hatch time, and fry feeding requirements (infusoria progressing to nauplii).

fish.breedingNotes: An egg scatterer that tends to spawn daily among fine-leaved plants, with eggs hatching in 6–10 days. Spawning is generally straightforward, but newly hatched fry are very small and require infusoria initially, making successful rearing the main challenge. Removing parents or transferring eggs daily to a separate vessel improves yield considerably.
